title: Catechist's Guide — The Virtues
html:
Catechist's Guide

To prepare and lead the class. For the exclusive use of the catechist.

Lesson Summary

Estimated duration: 50-60 minutes (with activities, 90 minutes)

Central theme: The virtues are habitual and firm dispositions to do the good. They are divided into human virtues (cardinal) —which we can acquire with effort and grace— and theological virtues (faith, hope, and charity) —which only God can infuse. The gifts and fruits of the Holy Spirit perfect and manifest the virtuous life.

Objectives:

  • That the student understands what a virtue is, as a habit that configures character.
  • That they distinguish the four cardinal virtues and know what each contributes.
  • That they understand why the theological virtues cannot be acquired through human effort alone.
  • That they recognize the role of the gifts and fruits of the Holy Spirit in the virtuous life.
  • That they identify which virtue they need to cultivate as a priority.

Key verse: 1 Corinthians 13:13 ("Faith, hope, love abide... the greatest is love")

Catechism references: CCC 1803-1845

Suggested Class Structure

Section Duration What the catechist does
1. Welcome and icebreaker 5 min Welcome, create a warm environment. Ask: "What quality do you most admire in a person?" Listen and connect to virtues.
2. The story of Thomas More 10 min Tell More's story in your own words. If you have the resources, show a clip from A Man for All Seasons (the trial scene or the conversation with his daughter in the cell). Ask: "What virtues do you see in him?"
3. Main content 22 min Present: (a) definition of virtue and acquired/infused distinction, (b) the four cardinal virtues one by one with examples, (c) the three theological virtues with 1 Cor 13, (d) gifts and fruits of the Holy Spirit. Read Wis 8:7 and 1 Cor 13:13 together.
4. Group activity 10 min Depending on age: children draw the Tree of Virtues. Youth and adults do the Virtue Check-Up (self-assessment 1-5).
5. Sharing 8 min Share (optional, don't force): "Which virtue did you discover you most need?"
6. Closing prayer 5 min Pray together the prayer to the Holy Spirit. Recall the memory verse from 1 Cor 13:13.

Notes for the Catechist

About Thomas More

St. Thomas More (1478-1535) was Chancellor to Henry VIII, a humanist, jurist, writer (author of Utopia), and father of a family. He was executed for refusing to sign the Act of Supremacy, which declared the king head of the Church of England. He was canonized in 1935 by Pius XI. St. John Paul II proclaimed him patron of statesmen and politicians in the year 2000. His feast is celebrated on June 22 (together with St. John Fisher).

More is one of the best examples for teaching the virtues because he lived all of them in an integrated way: prudence without cowardice, justice without rigidity, fortitude without recklessness, temperance without coldness, faith without fideism, hope without naivety, and charity without sentimentality. His process of discernment is a model of Christian prudence: he did not rush to martyrdom, exhausted every legitimate avenue, kept silence while silence was enough, and only spoke when remaining silent would have meant betraying the truth.

On the Distinction between Acquired and Infused Virtues

This is a theological point worth mastering. The distinction comes from St. Thomas (STh I-II, q.63). Not all students need the technical detail, but the catechist must understand it to answer questions. The essentials: the cardinal virtues can be acquired through the repetition of acts (with God's actual grace helping), while the theological virtues are always infused (poured out by God at baptism, without our acts being able to produce them). But God can also infuse supernatural cardinal virtues, which perform the same external acts as the acquired ones but with a radically different motivation and end: not health or human success, but union with God.

Comparative Table: Cardinal vs. Theological Virtues

Virtue Type Object Connection with God
Prudence Cardinal (human) Discern the concrete good and the means to achieve it Illuminated by faith, becomes practical wisdom seeking to please God
Justice Cardinal (human) Give God and neighbor what is due to them Extends to the virtue of religion (worship of God)
Fortitude Cardinal (human) Firmness and constancy in the good amidst difficulties Enables martyrdom: giving one's life for the faith
Temperance Cardinal (human) Moderate the attraction of pleasures Frees the soul for the contemplation of God
Faith Theological (infused) God himself as Truth who reveals himself Has God as its origin, motive, and direct object
Hope Theological (infused) God as future beatitude and present help Rests on God's promises, not on human strength
Charity Theological (infused) God loved for his own sake; neighbor loved for God Is the form of all virtues; without it, nothing avails (1 Cor 13)

On the Gifts of the Holy Spirit

The seven gifts (CCC 1830-1831, based on Is 11:2) are permanent dispositions that make the soul docile to follow the promptings of the Holy Spirit. It is important that students understand these are not for spiritual elites: every baptized person receives them. The difference lies in whether we activate them (cooperate with them) or leave them dormant. A useful image: the virtues are the oars; the gifts are the sails that catch the wind of the Spirit.

  • Wisdom: to savor the things of God, to see the world with God's eyes.
  • Understanding: to penetrate the truths of faith, to grasp their internal connection.
  • Counsel: to know what to do in concrete situations, beyond reasoning.
  • Fortitude: to overcome fear, to persevere to the end (distinct from the cardinal virtue).
  • Knowledge: to see created things in their relationship with the Creator.
  • Piety: filial love for God, trust in him as Father.
  • Fear of the Lord: not servile fear, but reverence that avoids offending the One we love.

On Adaptation by Age

Children (7-12): Use the tree metaphor and the athlete metaphor. Bring a thick rope made of several threads: each virtue is a thread; together they form a strong rope. The Tree of Virtues activity (Workbook, Activity A) works very well with them, especially if they can use colors. The example of Thomas More can be simplified: "He was a man who wanted to obey God more than the king, even though he knew they might kill him."

Youth (13-17): The testimony of Thomas More resonates strongly: an adult who does not let himself be swept along by social pressure. Connect with their daily experience: peer pressure, social media, the fear of being different. Temperance is especially relevant: mastery over screens, purity, moderation in consumption. The Virtue Check-Up (Workbook, Activity B) will give them an honest diagnosis. You can also mention Blessed Carlo Acutis: a young person who lived temperance in his use of technology.

New adults: The distinction between acquired and infused virtues often generates interesting questions. Lewis's argument that the cardinal virtues are "everyone's business" but the theological ones are "Christian" helps build bridges with those coming from outside. Don't be afraid of difficult questions: "Can an atheist be virtuous?" The answer is yes —in acquired virtues— but not in the fullness given by grace.

Formed adults: Go deeper into St. Thomas: the distinction between acquired and infused virtues (STh I-II, q.63), the connection between virtues and gifts (STh I-II, q.68), and charity as the form of the virtues (STh II-II, q.23, a.8). The theological debate on the "connection of the virtues" can also be introduced: can one have some virtues without others?

Theological Deepening for the Catechist

The Connection of the Virtues

A classic theme of moral theology is whether the virtues are "connected" among themselves (whether one who has one has them all, at least inchoatively). St. Thomas answers that the infused virtues are connected in charity, which is their form and root (STh I-II, q.65, a.2). But acquired virtues are not necessarily connected: you can be just without being particularly strong, or temperate without being especially prudent. Grace tends to unify them, but growth is gradual.

The Stoics, Aristotle, and Christian Virtue

Greek philosophy developed an admirable ethics of virtue. Aristotle defined virtue as a habit consisting in the mean (mesótes) between two vices. The Stoics identified virtue with reason and taught living "according to nature." But there are insurmountable differences with Christianity:

  1. The ultimate end: for the pagan, virtue leads to earthly happiness (eudaimonía). For the Christian, it leads to eternal beatitude (the vision of God).
  2. The source of strength: the Stoic trusts in his will; the Christian trusts in God's grace.
  3. The model: for the Stoic, the sage (sophós) is the ideal. For the Christian, Christ is the model. Christian virtues are not abstract qualities but participation in the life of Christ.
  4. Humility: the ancients did not consider humility a virtue; it was rather the opposite of magnanimity. Christianity places it at the center because pride is the root of all vices.

The Samurai, the Hagakure, and Christian Fortitude

The samurai code (Bushido), embodied in the Hagakure of Yamamoto Tsunetomo (18th century), exalts virtues that superficially resemble Christian ones: loyalty, honor, detachment from life, fortitude in the face of death. But the difference is absolute. The samurai prepares to die because death is the inevitable end; the Christian prepares to die because death is the gateway to resurrection. Christian fortitude is not Stoic resignation but certain hope: "If Christ has not been raised, your faith is futile" (1 Cor 15:14). The samurai dies with honor; the Christian dies with hope. As St. Paul said: "O death, where is thy victory?" (1 Cor 15:55).

Potential Difficult Questions (and How to Respond)

"Can an atheist be virtuous?"

Yes. The cardinal virtues can be acquired through human effort (aided by the actual grace God denies no one), and many non-believers possess them to a heroic degree. But full virtue —the kind that orders the person toward his supernatural end— requires the infused theological virtues and the supernatural cardinal virtues, which are received only through baptism and lived in the state of grace. Put differently: an atheist can be a "good person"; but to be a saint, one needs God.

"I don't have willpower. I'll never be virtuous."

No one has enough willpower. Christian virtues are not sustained by will but by grace. The one who thinks he can go it alone has already lost. The secret is not "try harder" but ask more: ask for grace, frequent the sacraments, cultivate prayer. Virtue is collaboration, not voluntarism. As Jesus said: "Apart from me you can do nothing" (Jn 15:5).

"Prudence is for cowards. Christians have to be bold."

Prudence is not cowardice. It is the intelligence that knows how to discern when, how, and why to act. The boldest saints were also supremely prudent. St. Paul did not rush into preaching without discernment: sometimes he stayed (Philippi), other times he fled (Damascus). Prudence is knowing how to read reality in the light of God. Without it, boldness is recklessness.

"Charity is the only thing that matters. Why complicate things with the rest?"

It is true that charity is the greatest of all (1 Cor 13:13). But to love well, you need all the others. To love with prudence (knowing when to speak and when to be silent). To love with justice (giving each person what they need, not what they ask for). To love with fortitude (persevering when love is costly). To love with temperance (without possessiveness, without unhealthy dependence). Charity is the queen; the other virtues are her court.

"The gifts of the Holy Spirit sound like something weird, like Pentecostal stuff."

The gifts of the Holy Spirit are not the patrimony of any particular group within the Church. They are firm Catholic doctrine (CCC 1830-1831) that goes back to Isaiah 11 and has been developed by the Fathers of the Church and St. Thomas. They are not extraordinary emotional experiences; they are stable dispositions that make the soul sensitive to the voice of the Spirit. Asking for them, receiving them, and cooperating with them is an essential part of the Christian life. Every baptized person has them. The question is whether they use them.

Supplementary Resources

  • YouCat 299-311: The section on the virtues is excellent for young people, with biblical citations, everyday examples, and personal reflection questions.
  • C. S. Lewis, Mere Christianity, Book III, Ch. 2: The cardinal virtues explained with clarity for skeptics and seekers. Highly recommended.
  • St. John Paul II, Veritatis Splendor: The moral encyclical par excellence. Develops the relationship between virtues, acts, and the person.
  • The Bible Project (YouTube): Videos on the Holy Spirit and the fruits of the Spirit. Animations of high theological quality.
  • Ascension Presents (Fr. Mike Schmitz): Episode "How to Grow in Virtue." Practical and motivating.
  • Word on Fire (Bishop Robert Barron): Excellent videos on virtue and character, available on YouTube.
  • Film A Man for All Seasons (1966): Available on streaming platforms and DVD. 120 minutes. Ideal for a complementary film-discussion session.

Optional Complementary Activity: Film Discussion

If you have a committed group and extra time (2.5 hours), consider organizing a film discussion around A Man for All Seasons. Questions for the follow-up discussion:

  1. In which moments of the film is More's prudence visible?
  2. How does he exercise justice as judge and as defendant?
  3. Where does his fortitude become apparent? And his temperance?
  4. The character of Richard Rich is the counterexample: what vices does he represent? How do they contrast with More's virtues?
  5. What role do faith, hope, and charity play in More's final decision?

The Catechist's Prayer

Lord Jesus, you who are the model of every virtue,
teach me to be prudent as you were,
never rushing or hiding,
but always discerning the Father's will.
Grant me your virtues
so that my students may see in me not a teacher
but a disciple of yours who tries, clumsily, to follow you.
May I not merely speak about you, but may you speak in me.
And may the Holy Spirit, with his gifts and fruits,
make fruitful this lesson I offer you today.
Amen.

Print Catechist's Guide
